## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with a video about creating build slides. So build slides are this magical thing that so many people just haven't figured out that you want to do. So this is a webinar killer. When you see all the points in a slide show showing up at the same time like this. And here's the second point plus the third points here and not to be laughed out. Here's the fourth point. And you may spend five minutes going through this slide and people are going, I'm gonna pass out versus this. It's the exact same data, but now each point shows up when you talk about it. Not before plus here's the third point and here's the fourth point. Can you see how that's a totally different experience for you as each point gets added as you talk about it? I'm going to tell you there are a few things more disrespectful to your audience than putting all your bullet points all up at the same time. So why use build slides besides that respect thing? First thing is it creates change, forcing their mind back to you for every point. So one of the things that we have found over and over again is that we is the human mind. When it looks at the same thing on the screen for a long time, it starts to get distracted like yours is right now. Your mind is looking for something else to look at because it's looked at this point for a while. And so if you put all the content on the page, all at once their mind is constantly being jerked around trying to decide what it's going to look at. Another reason why we do that, while we use build slides, is that if people are taking notes, it puts all the content on the page. And so if they didn't get the last one fully written out, they can finish writing it out before you go on to the next one. So you don't have this problem of, ah, can you go back to that last slide? ## [03:00 - 05:14]

Now watch what happens when I click this, it's going to fade up. So it doesn't just appear, it actually fades. I've got it set to fade in half a second. And so it doesn't take a long time to fade. You can set it so it'll fade for two minutes. It'll go. And finally show up. But I like a real fast fade, almost like it's just appearing. And this works really well on webinars. Remember, webinars have are bringing in bandwidth from you and then broadcasting that bandwidth out to others. They've got to they got to download and upload speed that they've got to deal with. And so they they almost always drop a bunch of frames in between. And so things tend not to be nice and smooth when it comes to animations and videos and those kinds of things. And so we need to keep that in mind as we look at the way we do these things because it doesn't always work well with it may look really cool. And you really love it, but it doesn't work well in webinars because of the effect on the system that got to happen to be able to broadcast. And so fade is still something different than just appearing, but it's not quite the same. So be careful of things that bounce around and flip over and blink because those are things that just don't work well on webinars. And realize that in most of these pieces of software, you also have the ability to do things like change the order of the animations, play with how long an animation takes to happen, play with the sequence of what comes after other things, all using something like in PowerPoint, it's called the animation pane. And in other programs, it'll have their own names, but that enables you to do things like, OK, I want this arrow to show up with that point, not with this point. And you can change all those things using the functions within your program. Strongly recommend. Use build slides. They're just so much more respectful for your people. They'll appreciate it.
